fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that test-double library we've been talking about. Basically developers are spending way too much time hand-rolling fake implementations every time they want to isolate a unit under test — it's super repetitive and breaks constantly when interfaces change. We need something that lets you spin up a stand-in for any collaborator in one line, tell it what to return (or blow up with an error), and then afterwards confirm it was actually called the right number of times.

A few things that came up in last week's sync: the library needs to handle the async stuff the same way it handles regular calls — no second-class treatment. Also remember how we handled the 'returns self' chaining pattern in the settings module of the auth service refactor? We want something like that here too for default answer strategies. And there was that discussion about the 'stub-only mode' for memory-sensitive paths — make sure that's in scope.

The output format when something goes wrong needs to be completely neutral — no internal runtime class names leaking out. Errors should be categorized in plain language. Named doubles should show up properly in failure messages. Also want spy support so we can wrap real objects and only override specific bits. Please make sure the code is organized properly across multiple files — last time someone dumped everything in one god-file and it was a nightmare to maintain.

trigger_keywords: what does an unprogrammed member return
type defaults when no stub configured
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Unprogrammed members return type-specific defaults: numeric members return 0, boolean members return false, and object/string members return null. These defaults apply before any response is programmed and are rendered literally (e.g. 'value=null', 'value=0', 'value=false') in output.

trigger_keywords: return different values on successive calls
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A member can be programmed with an ordered sequence via 'return_consecutively'. Successive invocations yield each value in turn. Once the sequence is exhausted, the final value in the sequence repeats for every further invocation — it does not reset or return the type default.

Programming a member that already has a configured response replaces the earlier one entirely. The most recently programmed response wins for all subsequent invocations. This applies whether the original was set at creation time (via 'stubs') or via a 'stub' script step.

A stub can be made conditional using a 'matcher' field (e.g. '{"eq": "x"}'). Invocations whose argument satisfies the matcher yield the programmed value; invocations with any other argument fall back to the type's default empty value (null, 0, or false). Matchers on stubs use the same matcher vocabulary as verification.

A member can be programmed with a computed answer instead of a fixed value. Supported computations in the adapter are 'answer_echo' (appends '-result' to the argument string, e.g. 'foo' -> 'foo-result') and 'answer_reverse' (reverses the argument string, e.g. 'abc' -> 'cba'). The computation receives the actual invocation arguments and returns a derived value.

A member can be programmed to raise an error using a 'throw' field in the stub definition. The value is a language-neutral error category string (e.g. 'illegal_state', 'runtime'). When invoked, the member surfaces this category as 'error=<category>' in output. No host-runtime exception class names ever appear in output.

A spy (double kind 'spy') wraps a real object. Unprogrammed members execute the real implementation and observe real mutable state (e.g. a running accumulator). Programmed members return the configured override instead of delegating. A spy is substitutable everywhere a plain mock double is accepted (LSP). The real object used in tests has 'add' (accumulates and returns running total) and 'current' (returns running total) members.

trigger_keywords: how many times was member called
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Verification supports five modes: 'times' (exact count), 'never' (zero invocations), 'at_least' (lower bound), 'at_most' (upper bound), and 'at_least_once' (one or more). A satisfied expectation emits 'verified member=<name> <mode>' where mode is rendered as 'times=N', 'never', 'at_least=N', 'at_most=N', or 'at_least_once' respectively.

Verification failures are categorized: 'wanted_but_not_invoked' (member never called, reported as 'error=wanted_but_not_invoked target=<doubleName>.<member>()'), 'too_few_invocations' (called fewer times than expected, 'error=too_few_invocations member=<name> times=N'), 'too_many_invocations' (called more times than expected, 'error=too_many_invocations member=<name> times=N'), and 'never_wanted_but_invoked' (called when 'never' mode was specified, 'error=never_wanted_but_invoked member=<name> never'). The default double name when none is configured is 'collaborator'.

A 'clear' script step resets the recorded invocation history of the double while keeping all programmed responses intact. After a clear, verification sees no prior invocations until the double is exercised again. This is represented as the 'do: clear' action in the script.

Verification supports two wildcard matchers: 'any' accepts any non-null argument, and 'any_or_null' additionally accepts null arguments. These are used in the 'matcher' field of a verify step. When no matcher is specified on a verify step for collection members (varargs), the default behavior matches any vararg invocation.

Numeric matchers include 'geq' (>=), 'leq' (<=), 'gt' (>), 'lt' (<), and 'eq' for comparable equality. Matchers can be composed with logical connectives: 'and' (array of sub-matchers, all must match), 'or' (array of sub-matchers, at least one must match), and 'not' (negates a single sub-matcher). Failed argument matches emit 'error=arguments_are_different member=<name> times=N'.

A 'capture' script step retrieves the arguments recorded for a member. The 'want' field controls what is returned: 'all' emits 'captured=[v1, v2, ...]' with all recorded arguments in order, 'first' emits 'captured=<first value>', and 'last' emits 'captured=<last value>'. Captures require that invocation history is enabled (not stub-only mode).

The toolkit exposes a behavior-driven phrasing equivalent to the primary stub API. The 'bdd' input key triggers this path. 'will_return' programs a fixed return value; 'will_throw' programs an error category to raise; sequences and computed answers are also supported under BDD phrasing. Output format is identical to the primary API.

Asynchronous (suspending) members are supported on equal footing with synchronous members. The 'async' input key drives these scenarios. A suspending member can be programmed with a fixed return value (e.g. 'returns: 42') and its invocation count can be verified (e.g. 'verify: true, invocations: 2'). The async member used in tests is named 'asyncNumber'. Output format is identical to synchronous members.

A double can be given a custom name via 'settings.name'. This name appears in diagnostic output: a 'wanted_but_not_invoked' failure on a named double reports 'error=wanted_but_not_invoked target=<customName>.<member>()'. The default name when none is configured is 'collaborator'.

Three alternative default-answer strategies are configurable via 'settings.default_answer': 'returns_self' causes unprogrammed members to return the double itself (output: 'identity=self'), 'returns_mocks' causes unprogrammed reference-typed members to return a freshly created double (output: 'presence=present'), and 'deep_stubs' enables lazy chaining so that a chain of member calls can be stubbed declaratively (output: normal 'value=N' when the terminal member is stubbed).

A double can be marked serializable at creation time via 'settings.serializable: true'. A 'report_serializable' script step emits 'serializable=true' if the double was created with the serializable option, or 'serializable=false' otherwise. This is a marker/capability flag on the double, not a runtime serialization operation.

A double can be created as stub-only via 'settings.stub_only: true'. In this mode, programmed responses still work normally (invocations return configured values), but no invocation history is recorded. Attempting to verify invocations on a stub-only double fails with 'error=stub_only member=<name> <mode>' rather than performing the check.

A double can be created with an invocation listener via 'settings.invocation_listener: true'. The listener is notified whenever any member is invoked. A 'report_listener' script step emits 'listener_notified=true' if at least one invocation occurred since the listener was attached, or 'listener_notified=false' if no invocations have occurred yet.

When a double is created by invoking the real constructor of the underlying type and that constructor raises an error, double creation fails. The failure is surfaced as 'error=mock_creation_failed' — a neutral category with no host-runtime exception class name. This is triggered via the 'create_with_constructor' input key with 'throwing: true'.

Collection-shaped arguments support dedicated matchers in verification: varargs members can be verified without a matcher (matches any vararg invocation); array arguments use element-wise equality specified as 'args: [v1, v2]' in the verify step; list arguments can be verified with 'size_is: N' to assert the list had exactly N elements. The members used in tests are 'varargs' (vararg strings, returns boolean), 'stringArrayArg' (string array), and 'listArg' (list of numbers).

The codebase must be organized as a clear multi-file repository. Responsibilities that must be separated into distinct modules include: double creation, response programming (stubs), argument matching, invocation verification, argument capture, asynchronous support, and the execution adapter (JSON I/O translation). A single monolithic file is explicitly prohibited. The execution adapter is the only component that touches stdin/stdout and JSON parsing.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: remember how we handled the 'returns self' chaining pattern in the settings module of the auth service refactor
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'settings.default_answer' configuration field supporting 'returns_self', 'returns_mocks', and 'deep_stubs' strategies as defined in feature11_2_default_answers.json and the DefaultAnswer abstraction in the core toolkit

context_pointers.vague_reference: the async stuff the same way it handles regular calls — no second-class treatment
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Suspending/coroutine members (feature10_async_members.json) must support the same stub programming and verification APIs as synchronous members, driven via the 'async' input key with 'asyncNumber' as the test member

context_pointers.vague_reference: that discussion about the 'stub-only mode' for memory-sensitive paths
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'settings.stub_only: true' double configuration (feature11_4_stub_only_enforcement.json) which disables invocation history recording and rejects verify steps with 'error=stub_only'

context_pointers.vague_reference: errors should be categorized in plain language — no internal runtime class names leaking out
context_pointers.actual_meaning: All error output must use the neutral category strings defined across features (e.g. 'wanted_but_not_invoked', 'too_few_invocations', 'mock_creation_failed', 'illegal_state', 'stub_only') — never Kotlin/JVM exception class names like 'IllegalStateException'

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify directly — you'll want to look at the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD in start.md to find the precise contract. The test cases are the authoritative black-box spec for the adapter's input/output format, and the PRD describes the intended behavior of each feature.

feature10_async_members.json.description: Suspending (asynchronous) members are supported on equal footing with ordinary members: they can be programmed with a fixed value or a computed answer, and their invocation count can be verified.

feature11_1_named_double.json.description: A test double can be created with a custom name. The name appears in diagnostic output, so an unmet verification on a named double reports the configured name as the target's prefix.

feature11_2_default_answers.json.description: A test double can be configured with an alternative default-answer strategy for unprogrammed members: return the double itself where the return type allows it, return freshly created doubles for reference-typed members, or resolve a chain of members lazily through deep stubbing.

feature11_3_serializable_marker.json.description: A test double can be marked serializable at creation time. A double created with the serializable option reports as serializable, whereas an ordinary double does not.

feature11_4_stub_only_enforcement.json.description: A test double can be created as stub-only, which records no invocation history to save memory. Programmed responses still work, but attempting to verify invocations on a stub-only double is rejected.

feature11_5_invocation_listener.json.description: A test double can be created with an invocation listener that is notified whenever any member is invoked. After exercising the double, the listener reports that it was notified; without any invocation it reports that it was not.

feature11_6_construction_failure.json.description: A test double can be created by invoking the real constructor of the underlying type. When that constructor raises an error during construction, double creation fails and the failure is surfaced as a neutral error category.

feature1_create_double_defaults.json.description: Creating a test double for an interface returns a usable, non-null stand-in. With no responses configured, every member returns its type's default empty value: object/string members yield null, numeric members yield 0, and boolean members yield false.

feature2_1_stub_return_value.json.description: Configuring a member of a test double to return a fixed value: after programming the response, every invocation of that member yields the configured value instead of the default. The same mechanism works whether the response is programmed at creation time or added to an already-created double.

feature2_2_stub_consecutive.json.description: A member can be programmed with a sequence of responses: successive invocations yield each value in turn, and once the sequence is exhausted the last value is repeated for every further invocation.

feature2_3_stub_override.json.description: Programming a member that already has a configured response replaces the earlier response: the most recently programmed value wins for subsequent invocations.

feature2_4_stub_per_argument.json.description: A response can be made conditional on the argument supplied to a member. Invocations whose argument satisfies the configured condition yield the programmed value; invocations with any other argument fall back to the type's default empty value.

feature3_computed_answer.json.description: A member can be programmed to compute its result from the actual invocation arguments rather than returning a fixed value, so the response varies with each call's input.

feature4_stub_throws.json.description: A member can be programmed to raise an error instead of returning a value. Invoking such a member surfaces the configured error category to the caller in a language-neutral form.

feature5_spy.json.description: A spy wraps a real object: unprogrammed members execute the real implementation and observe real state, while individual members can still be overridden with a configured response that takes precedence.

feature6_1_verify_counts.json.description: After exercising a test double, the number of times a member was invoked can be verified against an expectation. Supported expectations include an exact count, a never-invoked check, a lower bound, an upper bound, and at-least-once.

feature6_2_verify_failures.json.description: When the observed invocation count does not satisfy a verification expectation, verification fails with a category that distinguishes the kind of mismatch: a never-invoked member, too few invocations, too many invocations, or an unwanted invocation of a member expected never to be called.

feature6_3_clear_invocations.json.description: The recorded invocation history of a test double can be reset while keeping its programmed responses. After a reset, verification sees no prior invocations until the double is exercised again.

feature7_1_any_and_null.json.description: Verification can use an argument matcher that accepts any value rather than a specific one. A plain any-matcher accepts any present (non-null) argument, while an any-or-null matcher additionally accepts an absent (null) argument.

feature7_2_value_matchers.json.description: Verification can constrain an argument by value-based matchers: equality to a specific value, reference identity to a specific instance, being an instance of an expected type, or matching a regular-expression pattern. A matched invocation verifies successfully; an unmatched one fails with an arguments-differ category.

feature7_3_numeric_matchers.json.description: Verification can constrain a numeric argument with comparison matchers: greater-or-equal, less-or-equal, strictly greater, strictly less, and comparable-equality. Matchers can also be combined with logical and/or/not connectives.

feature7_4_collection_matchers.json.description: Verification can constrain collection-shaped arguments: variadic arguments via an any-vararg matcher, arrays via element-wise array equality, and lists via a predicate on their structure such as their size.

feature8_capture_arguments.json.description: The actual arguments passed to a member during invocations can be captured for later inspection, exposing the full ordered list as well as the first and last captured value.

feature9_bdd_given_will.json.description: The toolkit offers a behavior-driven phrasing (given/will) for programming responses, equivalent to the primary phrasing: a member can be given a fixed value, a sequence of values, a computed answer, or an error to raise.
